_comment: Phase 1c.x permanent regression fixture. DO NOT TUNE TO PASS — tune the substitution logic to handle this shape. See docs/migration-risk-register.md R-03.
name: WORST CASE SYNTHETIC NODE — 8 PHASES × 500-CHAR DESC × 1000-CHAR CUES
overview: Synthetic stress fixture for the Phase 1c template substitution and token-budget pipeline. This node intentionally maximizes per-phase content volume, scoring rules length, and template size to surface any code path that assumes Slant-shaped data. If this fixture passes in compact mode and produces a clean structured 413 in full mode, the substitution logic is shape-agnostic. If it silently truncates or crashes, the slice is not production-ready.
scoring_rules: Aggregate Score = Σ (metric_score_i × weight_i) for all metrics where confidence ≥ threshold. If scoring_renormalize_on_skip=true and any metric is skipped due to low confidence or excluded by analysis_context, redistribute that metric's weight proportionally across remaining metrics so total weight remains 1.0. Otherwise cap aggregate at sum of included weights. Phase score = weighted average of metrics whose primary phase matches that phase. Confidence-skipped metrics receive null score and are excluded from both phase and aggregate calculations. When all metrics in a phase are skipped, that phase scores null and is excluded from the phase average shown to the athlete. Apply min_metrics_threshold floor: if fewer than min_metrics_threshold metrics produced a score, the entire result is flagged 'insufficient data' and the athlete sees a guidance screen instead of a score. Tie-breaking on identical aggregate scores defers to the higher confidence_avg. All score values reported as integers 0-100; internal computation uses float64 throughout. Bilateral metrics use the higher-confidence side; if both sides exceed threshold, average them. Repeat across reps within a clip and report mean ± stddev — if stddev > 25% of mean, append a 'high variance' confidence flag.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 1 — Initial Setup and Pre-Movement Stance Establishment
phase_breakdown.description: The athlete begins in a balanced athletic stance with weight evenly distributed across both feet. Knees are flexed approximately 30 degrees, hips hinged slightly back, torso angled forward at 15-20 degrees from vertical. Hands sit relaxed at chest height, eyes are tracking the cue source, and the breathing is controlled. This phase is structural — no scoring metrics are computed here, but pose stability matters because any drift propagates into the launch phase and contaminates downstream velocity measurements. Common pre-movement faults include leaning too far forward, locking the knees, or initiating arm movement before the cue. The frame buffer overlaps slightly with the launch phase to ensure no transition frame is double-counted or missed.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Stack your ribs over your hips. Hands light, feet heavy. Keep the chin neutral — don't crane forward to see the cue, trust your peripheral vision. Pre-load through the inside of the foot, not the outside; the inside arch is where explosive lateral movement begins. Shoulders should feel like they are sitting on top of a coat hanger — relaxed, not pinched. Breath in through the nose, exhale right before the snap. If you feel tension in your jaw, your stance is too rigid; soften everything below the sternum. The single biggest tell of a high-level athlete in this phase is stillness — there is no fidget, no weight shift, no head bob. The body looks calm but every joint is loaded like a spring. Coaches: if you see the heels rocking or the hands resetting, the athlete is not actually pre-loaded — they are bracing, which slows the first step. Drill this in front of a mirror until the stance is identical rep over rep, then progress to closed-eye holds for proprioceptive depth.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 2 — Cue Recognition and Reaction Initiation
phase_breakdown.description: From the moment the cue fires to the moment the first measurable joint motion occurs (typically the heel lift or the hand drop). This window is dominated by the central nervous system — the muscles are responding to a decision that has already been made upstream. Reaction time is measured in milliseconds and varies dramatically with athlete level: elite reaction is sub-180ms, college is 180-220ms, high school is 220-300ms, youth often exceeds 300ms. Late reaction here cascades into every subsequent phase because the athlete is permanently behind on the rep. Phase boundary detection uses the first frame where any tracked keypoint exceeds a velocity threshold above the noise floor.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Reaction is trained, not innate. Drill it isolated — strobe lights, audio cues, partner-triggered visual cues — three times per session, ten reps each, with full rest between reps so the nervous system fires fresh. The mistake most athletes make is trying to be fast by tensing up; tension actually slows reaction because contracted muscle resists rapid change of state. The fastest reaction comes from a calm body that has practiced the response so many times it bypasses conscious processing. When you see the cue, do not think 'go' — your first step should already be happening. Eyes lead, hips follow, feet finish. If your first step is your dominant foot every rep, you have a tell — defenders read tells. Train both leads to symmetry. Coaches: video review the first 5 frames after the cue at 1/4 speed; the athlete who initiates with the head moves slow, the athlete who initiates with the hip moves fast. Cue the hip.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 3 — Initial Drive and First-Step Acceleration
phase_breakdown.description: The first three steps after the reaction. Force application is mostly vertical-into-horizontal here — the ground reaction force vector tilts progressively from 75 degrees from horizontal at step one to about 50 degrees by step three. Stride length is intentionally short and choppy to maximize ground contact frequency and minimize air time. Knee drive is high but not exaggerated; the goal is to get the foot back to the ground as fast as possible, not to lift it dramatically. Arm action is aggressive and opposite — opposite arm and leg drive together with full elbow flexion through the back swing.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Push the ground away. Do not reach for the ground with your foot — punch through it with your hip. The first three steps should feel like you are running uphill into a wall: low torso angle (about 45 degrees from horizontal), short hard steps, full arm action. Most athletes try to stand up too soon — they want to see where they are going. Resist that. The longer you stay in drive position, the more horizontal velocity you build, and the faster you get to top speed downstream. Common error: overstriding in step two because the athlete tries to recover from a slow step one. Don't chase speed with stride length — chase it with stride frequency. Cadence beats cover. Drill this with sled pulls at 30 percent body weight, 8 sets of 3 steps, focusing on staying down through the third step before letting the body rise into upright sprint mechanics.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 4 — Top-Speed Sprint and Mechanical Optimization Window
phase_breakdown.description: Athlete has reached upright sprint posture, foot strike pattern has stabilized into mid-foot landing under the center of mass, and stride length has expanded to its sustainable maximum. Vertical oscillation is the key efficiency marker — elite sprinters minimize bounce because every centimeter of vertical motion is energy not contributing to horizontal velocity. Cadence holds steady at approximately 4.5 to 5 strides per second. Arm action remains symmetric and powerful — hands rise to roughly chin level on the front swing and brush the hip pocket on the back swing.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Run tall. Eyes on the horizon, not the ground. Feel the contact under your hip, not in front of it — if you feel your foot landing ahead of your knee, you are braking with every step. The cue we use is 'silent feet': if the ground sounds like a drum, you are pounding; if it sounds like a whisper, you are gliding. Top speed is preserved by relaxation, not by trying harder. The face should be soft, the jaw unclenched, the shoulders down and back — any tension above the diaphragm leaks force out of the legs. Drill at 90-95 percent perceived effort, never 100, because 100 percent intent triggers the tension reflex that kills speed. Watch elite sprinters in slow motion — their faces look bored. That is not laziness; that is mastery.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 5 — Deceleration and Direction Change Preparation
phase_breakdown.description: Athlete reads the trigger to change direction and begins eccentric loading on the planned plant leg. The center of mass drops 10 to 15 cm. Hips sink back and outside — sit-down hip mechanics — to absorb forward momentum and pre-stretch the gluteus medius and lateral quad. Stride length shortens dramatically (often by 40 percent in two strides) and stride frequency holds. The lead arm drops to brace; the trail arm prepares to whip into the new direction.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Sink before you turn. Athletes who try to cut while still upright slip, miss the angle, or pull a hamstring. The eccentric load is the cut — the actual change of direction is just what happens after you load correctly. Feel your weight on the inside of the plant foot. Do not let the knee cave inward; track it over the second toe. The chest stays down through the load — if you stand up, you blow the cut. Drill 5-10-5 shuttles with attention only to the deceleration phase; the cut itself takes care of itself when the load is right. The eyes lead the new direction one beat before the body — that is what sells the move to a defender, not the foot.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 6 — Plant, Cut, and Direction Reversal
phase_breakdown.description: The plant foot strikes the ground at a deep angle (often 40-50 degrees from vertical for sharp cuts) and the entire kinetic chain redirects. Ground reaction force peaks here at 3 to 5 times body weight for high-level cuts. The plant knee absorbs and re-extends in roughly 100ms. The contralateral arm whips powerfully across the body to assist hip rotation. The torso rotates toward the new direction beginning before the plant foot leaves the ground — successful redirect requires upper body anticipation.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: The plant is violent and quiet at the same time. Violent in force, quiet in posture — no flailing, no overrotation. Push off the inside of the plant foot, not the toe. The toe is for sprint; the inside arch is for cut. Drive the opposite knee hard across the body to pull the hips into the new direction; the foot goes where the knee points. Most missed cuts are missed knees, not missed feet. The arm opposite the plant foot is the throttle — whip it. Athletes who keep both arms tight across the chest cannot generate rotational speed. Drill in sand or on a slight incline; both force the cut to be honest and expose any cheating with the upper body.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 7 — Re-Acceleration Out of Cut
phase_breakdown.description: First three steps after the redirect. Mechanically identical to Phase 3 (initial drive) but starting from a much lower velocity and a deeper torso angle. Stride length is short, ground contact time is long, arm action is aggressive. The athlete's task is to recover the velocity lost during the cut as quickly as possible — every frame of slow re-acceleration is a frame the defender or opponent gains ground.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Treat the cut like a starting block. The first step out of the redirect is a sprint start, not a continuation of the run. Get low, push hard, drive the knee, fire the opposite arm. Athletes who try to be fluid out of the cut are slow out of the cut — fluid looks pretty but force-into-the-ground is what creates separation. Drill exit speed with timing gates set 5 yards past the cut point; the athlete's job is to reach the gate as fast as possible after the redirect. If the time does not improve over weeks, the issue is upstream — the cut itself is too long, the load was wrong, or the athlete is standing up too early on the exit.

phase_breakdown.name: Phase 8 — Finish, Breakdown, or Catch Window Resolution
phase_breakdown.description: Final phase where the rep terminates — either with a catch, a breakdown into a defensive position, a continued sprint to a goal, or a controlled stop. Mechanics depend entirely on the rep purpose. For catch reps, the hands rise into ready position 2-3 frames before the ball arrives, eyes lock onto the ball, and the body adjusts micro-positioning to put the hands in the optimal frame. For sprint finishes, the lean comes only at the line — never before. For defensive breakdowns, the athlete drops into a controlled mirror position with feet wider than shoulders.
phase_breakdown.coaching_cues: Finish the rep with the same intent you started it. The most common error is mental letdown in the final phase — the athlete relaxes 5 yards early because the work feels done. It is not done until the rep is complete. Catch with your eyes, not your hands; if your eyes are off the ball, your hands cannot catch it no matter how good they are. Track the ball into the hands, not just toward the hands. For breakdown finishes, sit into the stance — do not rise out of the cut and then re-load; that double-pump costs you the play. Coach the finish as hard as you coach the start; the score is identical for either.
